Architecture/AI-Voice-Command-Flow.title: AI Voice Command Flow
Architecture/AI-Voice-Command-Flow.content:
AI Voice Command Flow

User speaks via microphone (ASCIA speaker, phone, or web interface)
Audio sent to ascia-ai’s Whisper for local speech-to-text
Transcript sent to local Ollama LLM with home context (entities, zones, user profile)
LLM produces structured response: natural-language reply + action (HA service call)
ascia-ai executes via HA REST (e.g., turn on lights, adjust thermostat)
Reply sent to Piper TTS for local speech synthesis
Audio plays on appropriate speaker(s)

End-to-end target: < 2 s without GPU, < 500 ms with dedicated GPU.

ascia-ai · Whisper · Ollama · Piper · M06 Local AI

Architecture/Camera-Video-Flow.title: Camera Video Flow
Architecture/Camera-Video-Flow.content:
Camera Video Flow

Camera streams RTSP continuously to ascia-nvr (Frigate + Go2RTC)
Go2RTC re-encodes RTSP → HLS for browser/mobile consumption
Frigate runs GPU-accelerated YOLO object detection on the stream
Detection events stored in NVR DB with metadata (object type, confidence, bbox)
Video segments written to data storage drive (continuous or event-driven)
Frontend requests HLS manifest from ascia-nvr API → plays adaptive stream via HLS.js
On detection, ascia-nvr publishes a notification event → HA Core → push notifications

Architecture/Data-Architecture.title: Data Architecture
Architecture/Data-Architecture.content:
Data Architecture
ASCIA OS uses multiple databases, each optimized for its data type and access pattern.
Databases
See Database Systems for the full table.
Primary data flows

Real-Time Device State Flow — Device → protocol handler → HA Core → WebSocket broadcast → frontend (3D), 3D engine, analytics, diagnostics.
Camera Video Flow — RTSP → Frigate (YOLO) → HLS via Go2RTC → frontend; detection events → HA Core → notifications.
AI Voice Command Flow — Mic → Whisper (transcribe) → Ollama LLM (with home context) → action via HA REST → Piper TTS → speakers.

Bus
MQTT Bus is the primary internal real-time message bus.

Architecture/Data-Privacy-Compliance.title: Data Privacy Compliance
Architecture/Data-Privacy-Compliance.content:
Data Privacy Compliance

GDPR: all personal data (camera recordings, presence tracking, voice) stored locally on the ASCIA server. No personal data transmitted to ASCIA Inc. or any third party without explicit consent.
Quebec Bill 25: privacy-by-design. Data collection minimized to what’s necessary for system operation. Customers can delete all their data any time.
ASCIA Cloud (optional): when enabled, the cloud relay handles only encrypted tunnel establishment. No user data transits ASCIA Cloud servers.
Tele-diagnostics (optional): when enabled by customer, only system health data (CPU, RAM, disk, device status, battery levels, network metrics) transmitted. No camera footage, voice recordings, or personal content.

Related

Architecture/Network-Architecture.content:
Network Architecture
ASCIA OS creates and manages a segmented network isolating IoT from the home — automatic, zero manual config.
Segments

First-boot sequence
On first boot ascia-network-manager:

Detects modem/router and home subnet
Creates IoT VLAN 100
Creates ASCIA-IoT SSID with auto-generated WPA3 password
Configures nftables: IoT cannot initiate to home; ASCIA can talk to both

Architecture/Real-Time-Device-State-Flow.content:
Real-Time Device State Flow

Physical device changes state (e.g., door sensor opens)
Protocol handler (Zigbee2MQTT, MQTT broker, ONVIF) detects the change
HA Core receives via integration framework
HA Core updates entity state in state machine + SQLite
HA Core broadcasts state_changed event on WebSocket API
ASCIA Frontend (WebSocket subscriber) updates 3D scene in < 100 ms
ascia-3d-engine receives event, updates internal scene state for new connections
ascia-analytics stores change in TimescaleDB
ascia-diagnostics monitors for anomaly (self-healing trigger)

Architecture/Update-Strategy.title: Update Strategy
Architecture/Update-Strategy.content:
Update & Deployment Strategy
ASCIA OS image build
GitHub Actions forks the latest validated HAOS image, injects ASCIA add-ons, pre-installs add-ons, packages the frontend. Output: single .img.gz flashed to NVMe with a standard imaging tool. First boot runs the wizard — operational in 5 min.
OTA updates















































TypeMechanismDowntimeFrequencyUser controlAdd-onDocker pull + container restartZero (rolling)As neededManual or autoFrontendNew SvelteKit build deployed to IngressZero (hot swap)As neededAutomaticHA CoreSupervisor-managed container update< 60 s rebootMonthly (after ASCIA validation)Manual approveHAOSFull OS partition swap + reboot< 90 s rebootQuarterlyManual approveCritical security patchEmergency pipeline, priority flagMinimalAs neededAuto-install + notify
HA Core update strategy
Version Freeze: ASCIA OS pins to a specific HA Core version. Updates only after internal regression testing (≥ 7-day validation window). Automated regression: CI runs full ASCIA integration suite against each HA release. Breaking change detection monitors HA deprecation notices. Rollback restores previous HA Core version within 10 min via supervisor downgrade. Communication: HA updates bundled into ASCIA OS releases — clients never see raw HA versions.
Rollback strategy
Auto-snapshot before every update. Add-on rollback via tagged Docker images. HAOS rollback via A/B partitions (auto-revert on boot failure).

Hardware/ASCIA-Proprietary-Hardware.title: ASCIA Proprietary Hardware
Hardware/ASCIA-Proprietary-Hardware.content:
ASCIA Proprietary Hardware
ASCIA’s vertically integrated device line. In development — not yet available for physical testing but designed to be drop-in replacements for equivalent third-party devices on the same protocols. Agencies are expected to architect the integration layer to be hardware-agnostic so ASCIA devices integrate as first-class citizens.
Devices

ASCIA All-In-One Sensor — Wi-Fi/PoE multi-sensor (temp, humidity, AQ, CO2, mmWave static + tracking, lux, BLE interceptor, microphone)
ASCIA Local Server — preconfigured mini-PC server (i7, 32 GB, 1 TB NVMe, integrated Zigbee coordinator)
ASCIA Alarm Panel — wired sensor panel (Konnected-equivalent)
ASCIA KNX Catalog — full KNX product line (planned)
ASCIA Zigbee Catalog — proprietary Zigbee sensors and actuators
ASCIA PoE Switch — wall-mountable scene controller / switch
ASCIA Tablet Dashboard — wall-mounted tablet
ASCIA PoE Actuator Modules — DIN-rail PoE actuators
ASCIA Audio Amplifier — multi-zone amplifier
ASCIA Wired Alarm Sensors — 22/2 wired sensors
ASCIA PoE LED Strip — PoE-powered LED
ASCIA Multifunction Relay — Ethernet relay
ASCIA Multifunction Thermostat — Ethernet multi-zone thermostat
ASCIA Door Lock — PoE/Ethernet smart lock
ASCIA Mini Dashboard — small wall display (NS Panel-equivalent)
ASCIA Wall Switch — full line of Ethernet wall switches

Agency expectations

Treat ASCIA proprietary as first-priority in discovery, stability, and feature completeness.
Provide firmware-level guidance where applicable.

Hardware/Hardware-Ecosystem-Overview.title: Hardware Ecosystem Overview
Hardware/Hardware-Ecosystem-Overview.content:
Hardware Ecosystem Overview
ASCIA OS supports a hybrid hardware ecosystem: 30+ partner brands for testing today plus the ASCIA proprietary line in development. The proprietary devices take priority in discovery reliability, stability, and feature completeness — they are designed to be drop-in replacements for equivalent third-party devices on the same protocol.
Major sections

ASCIA Proprietary Hardware — devices in development
Partner Brands — Aqara, Ring, Hue, Konnected, Sonoff, Reolink, Roborock, Sinope, Shelly, Sonos, Withings, ABB/MDT KNX, Ubiquiti, TP-Link, Eero, SMLIGHT, etc.
ASCIA Server — mini-PC server reference specs
Sensors · Lighting · Switches and Relays · Cameras · Robots
Curtains and Blinds · Thermostats · Smart Home Devices
Audio Systems · Multimedia
Network Infrastructure · KNX Systems
Protocols — full protocol matrix

Testing environment
All third-party devices listed are physically available at the ASCIA Lab in Montreal. Remote access via Tailscale after NDA + contract start. ASCIA proprietary devices are not yet available for physical testing but follow the protocols described.
Discovery priority
ASCIA proprietary devices must be detected and onboarded with highest priority and reliability in the discovery engine. Other brands use standard protocol-based discovery.
Related
ASCIA Proprietary Hardware · Partner Brands · Protocols · ASCIA Server · Vertical Integration

Hardware/KNX-Systems.title: KNX Systems
Hardware/KNX-Systems.content:
KNX Systems
ASCIA OS supports the complete KNX catalog from ABB KNX and MDT KNX — lighting actuators, blind/shutter actuators, HVAC actuators, scene controllers, push-button interfaces, binary inputs, logic modules, IP routers/gateways, energy meters.
Integration via KNX IP interface or KNX IP router on local network. Standard protocol: KNXnet/IP Core, KNXnet/IP Tunneling, KNXnet/IP Routing. Python library: xknx. Configuration via ETS file import.
KNX is Phase 2 (excluded from MVP per Origin Email). Agencies must demonstrate ETS, group address mapping, and KNXnet/IP tunneling experience explicitly.
The proprietary ASCIA KNX Catalog (planned) will mirror ABB/MDT.

Hardware/Protocols/BLE.title: BLE
Hardware/Protocols/BLE.content:
BLE
Bluetooth Low Energy. ASCIA uses BLE for room-level presence (the user’s phone broadcasts a BLE advertisement, ASCIA receivers measure RSSI to triangulate room) and as a less-expensive alternative to UWB. Compatible with Apple AirTag, Tile, generic BLE beacons. Managed by ascia-presence.

Hardware/Protocols/KNX.title: KNX
Hardware/Protocols/KNX.content:
KNX
Premium twisted-pair (TP) bus protocol used in commercial and luxury residential. ASCIA integrates via KNX IP interface or KNX IP router using KNXnet/IP (Core, Tunneling, Routing). Library: xknx Python. Configuration via ETS file import. Phase 2 — excluded from MVP. See KNX Systems.

Hardware/Protocols/MQTT.title: MQTT
Hardware/Protocols/MQTT.content:
MQTT
Message Queuing Telemetry Transport. ASCIA runs Mosquitto as a HA add-on — the primary internal real-time message bus. All MQTT traffic stays local; no messages forwarded to external brokers. Authentication: per-device username/password issued during onboarding. See MQTT Bus for topic patterns.
Related

Hardware/Protocols/Matter-Thread.title: Matter Thread
Hardware/Protocols/Matter-Thread.content:
Matter / Thread
Matter is the cross-vendor IoT application protocol; Thread is the underlying low-power mesh radio. ASCIA OS integrates via HA Core Matter integration (Phase 3-4) — Thread border router required. Auto-discovery via Matter commissioning < 30 s.
Related

Hardware/Protocols/ONVIF-RTSP.title: ONVIF RTSP
Hardware/Protocols/ONVIF-RTSP.content:
ONVIF / RTSP
ONVIF — Open Network Video Interface Forum, interoperability standard. RTSP — Real-Time Streaming Protocol, the underlying video transport. Auto-discovery on ports 80/8080/2020 < 20 s. Re-streaming to HLS via Go2RTC for browser/mobile playback. Critical for MVP via ascia-nvr.
Related

Hardware/Protocols/PoE.content:
PoE
Power and data over a single Ethernet cable. Standard for ASCIA wired devices: cameras, sensors, dashboards, actuators, LED strips. Discovery via LLDP + ASCIA MAC fingerprint. Port management via Omada/UniFi APIs (power consumption, port enable/disable, per-port VLAN). Critical for MVP.

Hardware/Protocols/UWB.content:
UWB
Ultra-Wideband radio. ASCIA installs 3–4 anchors per home; each resident carries a UWB badge or compatible phone. Mobile via Apple Nearby Interaction (iPhone 11+) and Android UWB API (Galaxy S21+, Pixel 6 Pro+). Real-time avatar in 3D plan. Person-specific automations. Phase 2/3 — excluded from MVP.

Hardware/Protocols/Wi-Fi.content:

Hardware/Protocols/Z-Wave.content:

Hardware/Protocols/Zigbee.title: Zigbee
Hardware/Protocols/Zigbee.content:
Zigbee
Mesh IoT protocol. ASCIA uses SMLIGHT coordinators (SLZB-06M and equivalents) connected over Ethernet (not USB). Zigbee2MQTT runs as a Docker add-on. ASCIA development must account for this network-coordinator architecture.
Mesh map, device states (online/offline/battery/firmware), OTA updates, advanced config (bindings, reporting intervals, calibration) exposed by Visual Protocol Management.
Critical for MVP. ~3000+ supported devices.

mmWave
Millimeter-wave radar. ASCIA mmWave sensors include two transmitters: one for static detection (sleeping/sitting) and one for dynamic position tracking (X/Y in room). Person counting, optional heart rate. Detection range 6–8 m. 1 sensor per room recommended. Compatible Aqara FP2; proprietary ASCIA All-In-One Sensor integrates mmWave.

Mobile-App/Flutter-Architecture.title: Flutter Architecture
Mobile-App/Flutter-Architecture.content:
Flutter Architecture
Clean architecture with clear separation between presentation, domain, and data layers.
Project structure
lib/core/                    — App config, constants, theme, routing
lib/features/dashboard/      — WebView integration, bridge handlers
lib/features/auth/           — Login, biometrics, sessions
lib/features/lidar/          — LiDAR scanning UI + state
lib/features/nfc/            — NFC/QR pairing flows
lib/features/notifications/  — Notification handling, channels, deep links
lib/features/location/       — Geofencing, GPS, presence
lib/features/ble/            — BLE beacon broadcasting/scanning
lib/features/camera/         — Camera viewing, intercom, clips
lib/features/offline/        — Local cache, command queue
lib/features/watch/          — Watch communication layer
ios/Runner/Plugins/          — Swift plugins (LiDAR, NFC, UWB)
android/app/src/main/kotlin/plugins/  — Kotlin plugins

Decisions

State management: Riverpod — compile-time safety, dependency injection, async-first.
Navigation: GoRouter — URI deep-link support; every screen has a unique path; push notifications include URI that GoRouter resolves directly.
Communication with ASCIA OS: WebSocket (HA WS API), REST (HA + ASCIA), HLS for video, WebRTC for intercom, mDNS for local discovery, FCM/APNs for push.

Native plugins
See Native Plugins for the full registry (ascia_lidar, ascia_nfc, ascia_uwb, ascia_ble_beacon, ascia_critical_alert).

Mobile-App/Mobile-App-Overview.title: Mobile App Overview
Mobile-App/Mobile-App-Overview.content:
Mobile App Overview
The ASCIA Mobile App is the primary customer-facing touchpoint of the ecosystem. It is NOT a simple WebView wrapper — it is a hybrid native app that combines the ASCIA web dashboard (rendered via embedded WebView) with deep native platform integrations that require direct access to device hardware and OS APIs.

























PropertyValueDocument ReferenceCDC-2026-ASCIA-MOBILE-001TechnologyFlutter + Dart, iOS + AndroidTargetiOS 15+, Android 10+ (API 29+)CompanionASCIA OS v1.0+
Why Flutter (not the HA Companion approach)
HA Companion uses native Swift/Kotlin + WebView loading Lovelace — adequate for technical enthusiasts. ASCIA targets premium non-technical customers and requires a fundamentally different mobile strategy: 100 % ASCIA branding, single codebase across iOS/Android (~40 % cost reduction), full Apple Watch + Wear OS support, native LiDAR/NFC/UWB plugins, custom rich notifications.

The two layers communicate via a bidirectional JavaScript Dart Bridge.

ASCIA hardware ships with NFC tag + QR code. Phase 2 — excluded from MVP.
Process

User holds phone near ASCIA device (NFC) or points camera at QR
App reads device identifier and type
App sends pairing request to ASCIA OS via REST
ASCIA OS confirms detection, returns status
App shows confirmation screen: name, type, suggested zone
User confirms or modifies zone/name
Device fully integrated, appears in 3D plan

iOS: Core NFC (NDEF foreground + tag-reader session)
Android: NFC adapter (NDEF discovery intent — foreground + background)
QR: mobile_scanner Flutter plugin
Tag format: NDEF JSON payload with device UUID, type, firmware, manufacturing date
Security: NFC payloads signed with ASCIA device certificates (anti-spoofing)

Native plugin: ascia_nfc (Swift CoreNFC + Kotlin NfcAdapter).

BLE Presence
Phone acts as a BLE beacon broadcasting unique ASCIA ID. ASCIA receivers (built into ASCIA sensors or dedicated beacons) detect signal, report RSSI; ASCIA OS triangulates approximate room. Room-level accuracy (not cm).

For premium installations. Centimeter-accurate indoor positioning.

Android: UWB API (Galaxy S21+, Pixel 6 Pro+)
Communicates with ASCIA UWB anchors
Avatar in 3D plan in real time
Person-specific automations

Mobile-App/Modules/M9-Offline-Mode.title: M9 Offline Mode
Mobile-App/Modules/M9-Offline-Mode.content:
M9 — Offline Mode & Local Cache
Cached dashboard
When ASCIA OS is unreachable (internet outage, out of VPN range), the app does not show a blank screen — it provides a degraded but functional experience.

Last-known device states cached locally (SQLite)
Cached dashboard displays states with stale indicator + last-update timestamp
Scenes can be queued — stored locally, executed when connectivity restored
Camera feeds unavailable offline (real-time streams can’t be cached)
Server-side automations continue independently

Local network priority
The app always prefers local over cloud/VPN:

On launch: check ASCIA OS via mDNS
If found locally: connect directly (< 10 ms)
If not: fall back to ASCIA Cloud relay or WireGuard
Automatic switchover: if user arrives home while using app remotely, connection transparently switches to local

OS/ASCIA-OS-Overview.title: ASCIA OS Overview
OS/ASCIA-OS-Overview.content:
ASCIA OS Overview
ASCIA OS is the proprietary smart-home OS built on top of Home Assistant OS. It inherits Home Assistant’s 3000+ device integrations while adding a complete value-added layer: branded UI, network security, advanced automations, surveillance, local AI, and diagnostics.
It is delivered as a complete disk image (.img.gz) flashed onto the ASCIA Server’s NVMe SSD. First boot runs the initialization wizard (network detection → user creation → device discovery) and the system is operational within ~5 minutes.
Architecture

Layer 1 — HAOS Foundation (unmodified upstream)
Layer 2 — Add-ons (~12 ASCIA Docker containers)
Layer 3 — Frontend SvelteKit (replaces Home Assistant Lovelace)

Full details: Three Layer Architecture.
Key principles

Zero coupling with Lovelace — end-user never sees Home Assistant branding.
Add-on isolation via Supervisor-managed Docker.
Local-first processing (AI, NVR, automations, storage).
API-first design — every add-on exposes documented REST/WebSocket APIs.
Security by default — automatic IoT isolation, encrypted communications, key rotation.
HA Core compatibility — tracks upstream WebSocket/REST APIs (Update Strategy).

Related
Three Layer Architecture · Stack Technique · Modules · Add-ons · Frontend SvelteKit · System Context

OS/Add-ons.title: Add-ons
OS/Add-ons.content:
Add-ons
Layer 2 of the Three Layer Architecture. Each ASCIA differentiator runs as a HA-Supervisor-managed Docker container in a private repo.
Standard pattern

Base image: Alpine 3.18+ (or Debian Slim) — < 200 MB target
Config: YAML + env vars from Supervisor
API: FastAPI (Python) or Express (Node.js) on dedicated port
Logs: structured JSON to stdout
Health: HTTP /health endpoint
Persistence: mounted /data volume
Tests: ≥ 70% unit + integration tests

Registry

ascia-network-manager — VLAN, firewall, IoT isolation
ascia-device-manager — universal device auto-discovery + onboarding
ascia-3d-engine — server-side 3D scene state management
ascia-nvr — Frigate + Go2RTC NVR
ascia-ai — Whisper + Ollama + Piper local AI
ascia-automation-ai — natural-language automation creation
ascia-audio — multiroom audio (Music Assistant)
ascia-presence — mmWave + BLE + UWB
ascia-analytics — TimescaleDB + Grafana energy & history
ascia-vpn — WireGuard
ascia-diagnostics — Prometheus + self-healing + tele-diagnostics
ascia-backup — Rclone-based backups

Inter-add-on communication

Add-on → HA Core: REST/WebSocket
Add-on → Add-on: REST on Docker bridge network
Add-on → Frontend: WebSocket push
Add-on → Devices: MQTT / protocol-specific
Add-on → External: HTTPS outbound (weather, Waze, cloud backup)

Related
Three Layer Architecture · MQTT Bus · CI CD Pipeline · Stack Technique

OS/Frontend-SvelteKit.title: Frontend SvelteKit
OS/Frontend-SvelteKit.content:
Frontend SvelteKit
Layer 3 of the Three Layer Architecture. A SvelteKit single-page application that completely replaces Home Assistant’s Lovelace dashboard. Served by HA Supervisor’s Ingress on port 443.
Stack













































ComponentTechnologyResponsibilityFrameworkSvelteKit + TypeScriptRouting, SSR, build pipeline3D EngineThree.js + WebGL3D Dashboard renderingState ManagementSvelte stores + WebSocket subscriptionsEntity states, UI state, 3D sceneReal-time CommunicationHA WebSocket API + ASCIA WebSocketEntity streaming, command executionAPI ClientFetch API with typed wrappersREST callsVideo StreamingHLS.js + native <video>Camera live + recordingsDesign SystemASCIA tokens + componentsBranding, dark/light themes
Principles

100% ASCIA branded — no Home Assistant logo, terminology, or visual identity.
Mobile-first responsive layout.
Perceived latency target < 100 ms for local actions.
Accessibility WCAG AA, French/English bilingual.
No direct database access — all data via APIs.

Build & deploy

Build: SvelteKit production bundle, < 2 MB gzipped (CI gate).
Lighthouse: Performance ≥ 80, Accessibility ≥ 90.
Deployment: hot-swap into Ingress with zero downtime.

OS/HA-Core.title: HA Core
OS/HA-Core.content:
HA Core
The Python (3.11+ async) backend embedded in HAOS Foundation. ASCIA components interact with HA Core exclusively through its published APIs — no direct access to HA’s internal Python modules or database.
Exposed APIs (consumed by ASCIA)

HA WebSocket API (wss://) — real-time entity state subscriptions, command execution, automation triggers. Primary consumer: Frontend SvelteKit.
HA REST API (HTTPS) — one-off operations: entity state queries, service calls, configuration reads. Primary consumer: Add-ons.
HA Supervisor API (HTTPS, internal) — add-on management, system info, snapshots. Auto-injected supervisor token.
HA Event Bus — event-driven triggers: state changes, device registration, automation execution.

Authentication: long-lived Bearer tokens.
Concepts

Entity — logical representation of a device or attribute (e.g., light.living_room, sensor.door_bedroom1).
Blueprint — configurable, reusable automation template.

Related

OS/HAOS-Foundation.title: HAOS Foundation
OS/HAOS-Foundation.content:
HAOS Foundation
Layer 1 of the Three Layer Architecture. HAOS is a minimal Buildroot-based Linux distribution. ASCIA uses it without modification to benefit from upstream security patches, driver updates, and new HA integrations.
Components

Linux Kernel (Buildroot custom) — drivers, nftables firewall, network stack
HA Supervisor (Python + Docker) — container lifecycle, add-on installation/updates, snapshot/restore
HA Core (Python 3.11+ async) — entity state machine, automation engine, integration framework
HA Database (SQLite) — entity state history, event log
HA Authentication (Python built-in) — accounts, tokens, session management
Container Runtime — Docker CE (isolation for all add-ons)
Network Stack — NetworkManager + systemd-networkd

Why unmodified
Forking HA Core would lose access to its 3000+ integrations and 8 years of community development. Apache 2.0 license explicitly permits commercial products. ASCIA tracks HA Core releases via version freeze + regression testing.
Related
HA Core · Three Layer Architecture · Add-ons · Update Strategy · Open Source Licenses

M01 — Network & Infrastructure
Add-on: ascia-network-manager · Priority: P0 Critical · Excluded from MVP per Origin Email.
On first boot ASCIA OS analyzes the environment and configures all network security with zero user intervention:

Detects existing modem/network
Creates dedicated IoT VLAN 100 isolated from the home network
Creates a separate ASCIA-IoT Wi-Fi SSID with auto-generated WPA3 password
Configures nftables firewall: IoT cannot initiate connections to home network
Deploys internal ASCIA DNS for IoT device-name resolution
Configures DHCP with reserved IP ranges per device type (cameras 10.100.1.x, sensors 10.100.2.x)
Activates basic IDS rules monitoring anomalous IoT behavior

Dynamic management

New device → automatic profile + safety rules.
Removed device → rules deleted.
Continuous monitoring + weekly network report.
Compatible scenarios: basic modem, Ubiquiti UniFi, TP-Link Omada, pfSense/OPNsense, manageable switch.

Visual management
Visual Protocol Management surfaces network dashboard, network map, firewall rules, VLAN admin, history, smart alerts.

M06 — Local Artificial Intelligence
Add-on: ascia-ai · Stack: Whisper + Ollama + Piper · Priority: P1. Excluded from MVP per Origin Email.
Philosophy
100% local. No data from the home (voice, questions, statuses) ever leaves the ASCIA server. AI is built for one specific home, learns its occupants’ habits, becomes more relevant over time.
Conversational features
Examples:

“Is the oven on?” → “Yes, kitchen oven for 23 min at 180 °C”

Client describes in natural language: “Turn on living-room lights when I get home” → automation generated. AI asks up to 10 clarifying questions for complex automations · validation step before activation · suggests variations.

Morning report (auto/on-demand): motion events, returns, oven status. Read on house speakers. Proactive suggestions (“It’s raining and your office window is open. Close it?”). Pattern-based suggestions (“You always turn off the kitchen lights after 11 pm — automate it?”).

M07 — Presence & Localization
Add-on: ascia-presence · Priority: P1. UWB is Phase 2 and excluded from MVP.
Proprietary mmWave sensors
ASCIA produces its own mmWave sensors (inspired by the Everything Smart Presence Sensor One format) with two transmitters — static detection (sleeping/sitting) + dynamic position tracking. Compatible with Aqara FP2 as fallback.
Features: static presence · dynamic X/Y in room · person count · optional heart rate · zone delineation in 3D plan · zone exclusion · guided calibration · sensitivity adjustments.
UWB system
Centimeter-level localization (< 30 cm). 3–4 anchors per home, badge or key fob per resident. Real-time avatar in 3D. Person-based automations: “When Jean-Pierre enters the kitchen → start coffee maker with his profile.”
BLE
Less precise alternative to UWB. Detection via the customer’s phone or dedicated Apple AirTag, Tile, generic BLE tags. ASCIA offers BLE (economical) and UWB (premium) options.

OS/Modules/M14-Diagnostics-Self-Healing.title: M14 Diagnostics Self Healing

M14 — ASCIA Diagnostics, Self-Healing & Remote Support
Add-on: ascia-diagnostics · Priority: P0 for ASCIA customers. Excluded from MVP (sections 14.1, 14.3, 14.4) per Origin Email.
Automatic proactive detection























































ConditionAlertAuto-actionBattery < 15 %Customer pushReplacement suggestionStorage > 80 %UI alertArchival/extension suggestionDevice offline > 5 minCustomer pushPing + service restartServer overheatingCriticalReduced load + ASCIA support alertHouse overheating > 35 °CCustomerVentilation suggestionNetwork latency > 500 msUIAuto network diagSSL cert expires in 30 daysAdminAuto-renewal attemptBackup failedAdminRetry + notify if repeatedOutdated firmwareInfoOTA proposal
Self-Healing — automatic recovery
ASCIA OS attempts to fix problems automatically before alerting the customer or creating a ticket. Three-tier system (see Self Healing Tiers):

Tier 1 Soft: 2 health-check failures → restart container · silent log
Tier 2 Service: Tier 1 fails 3× → rebuild container + data check + reconnection · “Maintenance performed, services restored”
Tier 3 Escalation: Tier 2 fails → ticket with diagnostic bundle · “Issue detected, ASCIA support notified”

On-demand self-diagnosis
Complete test on-demand or scheduled: per-device ping/latency, automation testing, network test, server test (CPU/RAM/temp/disk). Health score 0–100 with recommendations.
ASCIA Tele-Diagnosis
With explicit consent, daily/weekly diagnostic report sent to ASCIA team. Only system metrics — no personal data, no images, no voice recordings. Enables proactive intervention before customer notices. Revocable any time.

M18 — ASCIA Business Process & Deployment
The business process is fully integrated with the OS to maximize efficiency and minimize errors. See Sales Process for the complete stage-by-stage table.
Workflow overview

On-site submission — ASCIA technician with ASCIA POS tours the property, lists rooms/zones, captures all desired devices. Names of parts and areas are entered now.
Transmission & logistics — Validated submission → logistics manager → parts ordered → ASCIA server pre-configured with submission data (zones, expected device types).
Installation — Customer receives: ASCIA server + PoE switches + devices. Connect: modem → switch → server + devices.
Guided client configuration — Upload/create 3D plan, devices appear automatically, customer assigns names + positions in minutes, basic automations ready.
Continuous support — Proactive remote diagnostics, temporary support access with consent, automatic ticketing, remote firmware updates.

OS/Three-Layer-Architecture.title: Three Layer Architecture
OS/Three-Layer-Architecture.content:
Three Layer Architecture
The fundamental architectural decision of the project. Each layer has distinct responsibilities, ownership, and update cycles.

































LayerTechnologyRoleOwned by ASCIAUpdate SourceL1 — FoundationHAOS Foundation (Linux + Buildroot + HA Supervisor + HA Core)OS, container orchestration, state machine, 3000+ integrationsNo — unmodified upstreamHA monthly releasesL2 — Add-ons~12 Docker containers (Python, Node.js, Go)ASCIA differentiators: network, device discovery, NVR, AI, diagnosticsYesASCIA private repoL3 — FrontendFrontend SvelteKit + TypeScript + Three.jsBranded UI replacing Lovelace: 3D dashboard, cameras, automations, settingsYesASCIA private repo
Why three layers

L1 inherits HA’s mature ecosystem and security patches without ASCIA forking the codebase.
L2 isolates each ASCIA feature into its own container — failures don’t cascade.
L3 owns 100% of the user experience; the customer never sees Lovelace.

Communication

Frontend → HA Core: WebSocket + REST.
Frontend → Add-ons: REST endpoints.
Add-on → Add-on: REST on Docker bridge network.
Add-on → Devices: MQTT / protocol-specific APIs.
See Data Architecture and APIs.

Open-Questions.title: Open Questions
Open-Questions.content:
Open Questions
Items where the source documents are inconsistent, incomplete, or imply decisions that are not explicit. Each one is worth raising with ASCIA before finalizing the proposal or implementation.
Scope contradictions
1. Network manager: MVP or excluded?

Origin Email explicitly excludes “Section 1.3 — Isolated secure network (auto VLAN/firewall)” from MVP.
OS Specifications roadmap §21 lists ascia-network-manager v1 in Phase 1 (MVP) with weeks 2–6.
Tech Architecture classifies ascia-network-manager as Phase 1 (MVP).
Mobile App spec assumes mDNS discovery on the local network, which presupposes a working ASCIA-managed network.
Resolution needed: which trumps? If §1.3 is genuinely excluded, the MVP cannot deliver Plug & Play as advertised. Ask ASCIA which subset of M01 Network and Infrastructure is in scope.

2. Self-healing scope in MVP

Email excludes Sections 14.1, 14.3, 14.4 (Auto-diagnostic, Self-Healing, Tele-Diagnostic).
Tech Architecture lists ascia-diagnostics as Phase 1 MVP.
Likely answer: basic monitoring + Prometheus collection is MVP; the 3-tier auto-recovery logic and tele-diagnostics opt-in are post-MVP. Confirm.

3. Module numbering
The Vision document numbers 18 modules. The Specifications document restates 16 (modules 17 and 18 — Nomenclature and Business Process — appear in Vision but not as numbered modules in Specs). The vault uses Specs numbering (M01–M16) plus M17/M18 from Vision. Confirm canonical numbering.
Technical ambiguities
4. Zigbee coordinator: USB vs network

Hardware Ecosystem §15 states ASCIA uses SMLIGHT SLZB-06M over Ethernet — agencies must account for this.
Tech Architecture §6.2 recommends SONOFF Zigbee 3.0 USB Dongle Plus (CC2652P).
Both devices appear under “ASCIA reference recommendations”. Which is the canonical MVP coordinator? Architecture must support either; clarify default.

5. ASCIA proprietary vs third-party priority
Hardware doc says ASCIA proprietary devices “must be detected and onboarded with the highest priority and reliability in the discovery engine” — but ASCIA proprietary devices are not yet manufactured. How is “first-priority” enforced in MVP if no proprietary hardware is available for testing?
6. 3D engine: client-side vs server-side

ascia-3d-engine (Layer 2) handles “server-side 3D scene state management”.
Frontend SvelteKit handles WebGL rendering via Three.js.
The split is described but the contract between them isn’t fully specified — what travels over ws://subscribe? Full GLB updates or just entity → 3D-object delta? Affects bandwidth, mobile WebView feasibility.

7. BLE on iOS background
Mobile spec acknowledges iOS Core Bluetooth restrictions on background advertising → falls back to iBeacon region monitoring. iBeacon is receive, not advertise. The architecture (phone-as-beacon) cannot work as described on iOS in background. Resolution needed: is the phone the receiver (ASCIA infrastructure broadcasts) or the broadcaster?
8. Update flow vs MVP
Email excludes “Section 15.3 Update management system” from MVP, but Tech Architecture’s Update Strategy is foundational for stability. Likely meaning: end-user-facing update UI is post-MVP; the underlying OTA pipeline must still exist for ASCIA to ship updates. Confirm.
Process / commercial
9. Two proposals or one?
Email states the agency must submit both a “MVP only” proposal and a “complete project” proposal. RFP Section 6 implies a single proposal. Confirm format and pricing structure.
10. Phase 0 mini validation contract
Elimination criterion mentions “refusal to begin with a mini validation contract (Phase 0)” — but Phase 0 is described in OS Spec §21 as 4 weeks of preparation (HAOS fork, CI/CD, design system). Clarify: is Phase 0 a separately contracted paid validation, or rolled into the main contract?
11. UI/UX coverage
Email says “Figma covers the customer-facing UI; configuration/admin screens are open for the agency’s proposal”. Need to know: which screens are designed in Figma, which are not? A mapping per module / page would prevent overrun.
Hardware / lab
12. Lab access timing
Email and Hardware doc state the Montreal Lab is accessible via Tailscale “after NDA signing and contract start”. RFP requires Section 6.2 evidence of working hardware integration before contract start. Resolution: which production hardware can be tested pre-contract?
13. ASCIA proprietary hardware ETA
Devices in ASCIA Proprietary Hardware are listed as “In Development” — no ETA is given. The MVP roadmap does not depend on them, but Phase 2/3 features (ASCIA-priority discovery, ASCIA mmWave precision, ASCIA All-In-One Sensor-based AI mic input) implicitly do. Need a target availability date per device.
Compliance / legal
14. Quebec Bill 25 specifics
Compliance is asserted but no specific privacy-impact assessment or data-deletion API is described. The ASCIA Cloud relay’s exact data handling vs Bill 25 needs clarification.
15. Apple Critical Alert Entitlement
Mobile spec depends on this entitlement (bypass DND for fire/water/intrusion). Apple requires explicit justification and grants it sparingly. Has ASCIA started the request? It’s a long-lead-time item.
Documentation gaps
16. ASCIA POS system
Sales Process, M17 Nomenclature, and M18 Business Process all reference “ASCIA POS” as a known system. No specification document for it has been provided. Whose responsibility is the POS — ASCIA, the agency, or out-of-scope?
17. ASCIA Support Platform
Same as POS — referenced as an integration target in M16 External Integrations (priority P0) but not specified.
18. List of 179 features
Email mentions a “List of 179 features with MVP/Post-MVP/V2.0 classification and effort estimates” provided after NDA. Without it, MVP scope cannot be priced precisely. Request immediately.
Related
MVP Scope · MVP Exclusions · Origin Email · Roadmap and Phases

RFP/Elimination-Criteria.title: Elimination Criteria
RFP/Elimination-Criteria.content:
Immediate Elimination Criteria
If any of these is not met, the application is automatically eliminated regardless of the rest.

No public/private code for key profiles — without proof of actual code, technical quality cannot be assessed
Estimate delivered in < 72 hours after receipt — a serious quote needs ≥ 5 days analysis
Proposal makes no mention of technical risks — a partner who doesn’t see difficulties cannot manage them
No profile capable of creating native Flutter (Swift/Kotlin) plugins — essential for mobile native features
No developer capable of working on embedded Linux + Docker — the OS image and add-ons absolutely require it
Request for > 25 % of total budget as initial deposit — reputable partners charge on delivery, not signature
Refusal to begin with a mini validation contract (Phase 0) — if the agency isn’t confident proving its value, that’s a signal
No availability for minimal Montreal time overlap — coordination needs ≥ 3 hours simultaneous work daily

MVP Scope
ASCIA OS MVP includes

Frontend SvelteKit base replacing Lovelace (WS login, basic pages)
ascia-network-manager v1 (auto isolation, VLAN, basic firewall) — note: Vision §1.3 originally excluded; the OS Spec roadmap lists it as Phase 1
ascia-device-manager v1 (PoE, Zigbee, Wi-Fi, ONVIF self-discovery)
Automatic nomenclature (zone + type)
Basic NVR (ascia-nvr v1) — Frigate, continuous recording, stream viewing
3D Interface v1 — basic rendering, real-time states for lights/doors/blinds
Backup System v1 (ascia-backup)
ascia-vpn (WireGuard)
ascia-diagnostics basic
Testing + stabilization

Mobile App MVP includes

Flutter project setup with clean architecture, Riverpod, GoRouter
WebView integration with flutter_inappwebview, JS-Dart bridge, token injection
Auth: biometric (Face ID/fingerprint), PIN, username/password
Server discovery via mDNS on local network
Basic push notifications (text + sound, no images/buttons yet)
Geofencing: home zone enter/exit with automation trigger
Offline cache: last-known states displayed when server unreachable
Camera live view: single-camera HLS stream
Settings: account, logout, server connection info

MVP protocols
Mandatory: Zigbee (Zigbee2MQTT), ONVIF/RTSP, MQTT, Wi-Fi (mDNS/SSDP), PoE (Omada API), WireGuard VPN.
Delivery
ASCIA OS installable and testable by the ASCIA technical team. First pilot at a real customer site occurs in Phase 2.

RFP/Roadmap-and-Phases.content:
Roadmap & Development Phases
Phase 0 — Preparation (4 weeks)

Dev environment setup (macOS + Linux for HAOS)
HAOS fork + private ASCIA OS repo
CI/CD setup

Internal ASCIA API definition (interface contract)
Operational dev ASCIA server with basic HAOS

Team: 2 devs (senior full-stack + DevOps). Deliverable: infrastructure & basic tools.
Phase 1 — MVP Foundations (Months 1–4)
Frontend SvelteKit base · ascia-network-manager v1 · ascia-device-manager v1 · automatic nomenclature · basic NVR (ascia-nvr v1) · 3D Interface v1 · Backup System v1 · testing & stabilization.
Team: 3 devs (2 full-stack + 1 DevOps). Deliverable: ASCIA OS testable by ASCIA technical team.
Phase 2 — Advanced Features (Months 5–9)
Local AI v1 (chatbot, Whisper, Ollama) · 3D Interface v2 (RGB, presence, robots, thermal) · mmWave v1 + 3D mapping · auto-blueprints/groups · protocol interfaces (KNX, Z2M, PoE/Wi-Fi, Omada/UniFi visuals) · self-healing v1 · advanced multi-user · reports & events · pilot tests at 3–5 customer sites.
Team: 4–5 devs (3 full-stack + 1 AI + 1 DevOps). Deliverable: first pilot installation at customer site.
Phase 3 — Commercial Platform (Months 10–15)
Autopilot mode + presence simulation · AI v2 (suggestions, morning report, wake word) · UWB system · remote diagnostics + ASCIA support portal · Google Home, Alexa, Calendar, Waze · advanced network mgmt · advanced energy with Hydro-Québec · local facial recognition (opt-in) · Flutter mobile app v1.0 (App Store + Google Play).
Team: 5–6 devs + 1 QA + 1 PM. Deliverable: ASCIA OS v1.0 — available to all customers.
Phase 4 — Continuous Innovation (Months 16–24)
Public ASCIA SDK · multi-property mode · AI v3 (deep learning of habits, advanced predictions) · full Matter/Thread integration · hotel module · public ASCIA API for certified third-party developers.
Team: 6–8 devs + 1 QA + 1 PM. Deliverable: ASCIA OS v2.0 — Reference Platform.
Mobile alignment
The mobile app development is synchronized with ASCIA OS:

Phase 1 — MVP (months 3–6): WebView dashboard, auth, basic notifications, geofencing, local network discovery, offline cache
Phase 2 — Advanced (months 7–10): LiDAR, NFC, BLE, rich notifications, camera intercom
Phase 3 — Commercial (months 11–14): Apple Watch + Wear OS, UWB, full offline, App Store + Google Play

RFP/Technical-Questions.title: Technical Questions
RFP/Technical-Questions.content:
Technical Questions — Required Answers
Vague or generic answers count as inability to answer. Honest answers preferred over fabricated competence.
System architecture

Managing HA Updates — strategy for (a) freezing HA for stability, (b) testing new HA without breaking installs, (c) migrating add-ons on incompatible HA. ≥ 200 words.
Physical Hardware Tests for IoT — how to (a) procure/access test equipment, (b) structure hardware integration tests, (c) manage cross-brand behavior differences on the same protocol. ≥ 150 words.
3D Engine Performance — Three.js optimization for 200+ entities at 60 FPS without dedicated GPU; updating 200+ states without blocking main thread; auto degraded mode. ≥ 200 words.
Network Self-Discovery Architecture — automatic PoE detection via Omada/UniFi API; conflict handling when same device detected by multiple mechanisms (PoE + ONVIF + mDNS); auto-identifying ASCIA device type from network profile. ≥ 200 words.
Automatic Network Isolation — interacting with Omada/UniFi APIs to programmatically create VLANs and firewall rules; fallback when client switch isn’t Omada/UniFi; rollback if rule cuts critical connectivity. ≥ 200 words.

Process & quality

Managing the HA Learning Curve — week-by-week onboarding for devs to master HA Core in 4 weeks; who drives skill development and how it’s measured; strategy for accessing external HA expertise when stuck. ≥ 150 words.
Code Quality and Documentation — Python standards (lint, typing, coverage); how docs are written during development not after; code-review process and final-say authority; concrete past quality issue caught and fixed. ≥ 200 words.
Project Risk Management — 5 most significant technical risks seen + mitigation per risk. ≥ 300 words.
Communication and Jet Lag — concrete daily plan (standups, tools, times); urgent-blockage protocol outside common hours; how ASCIA stays involved in architecture decisions without slowing the team. ≥ 150 words.
Crisis Scenario — month 3, senior frontend Three.js dev resigns with 2 weeks’ notice, 3D 60 % done. Action in first 48 h; knowledge transfer; replacement profile and timeline; impact on schedule/budget; client communication. ≥ 200 words.

Vision/Plug-and-Play-Philosophy.title: Plug and Play Philosophy
Vision/Plug-and-Play-Philosophy.content:
Plug and Play Philosophy
The customer experience target: plug it in, turn it on, the system does the rest. No network professional, no IT technician, no VLAN configuration, no command line.
Ideal installation

Connect modem → main switch → ASCIA server.
Connect all devices to the switches.
Open the ASCIA interface from a phone or browser.
Initial guided setup (name, language, geographic area, 3D plan).
Devices appear automatically, ready to configure.

The customer never touches their existing modem, router, or VLAN settings. ASCIA OS creates everything downstream automatically via ascia-network-manager.
Related
Self Discovery · Network Architecture · ASCIA Server · Sales Process · Product Vision

Vision/Product-Vision.title: Product Vision
Vision/Product-Vision.content:
Product Vision
ASCIA OS is a next-generation home-automation operating system distributed as a pre-installed disk image on a dedicated ASCIA Server. It is a fork of Home Assistant OS enriched with a proprietary stack — Frontend SvelteKit interface, Docker add-ons, real-time 3D Dashboard, self-discovery, and Local AI — all delivered as a turnkey appliance.
Where Home Assistant targets enthusiasts and developers, ASCIA targets the non-technical premium customer who wants the system to “just work” the moment it is plugged in.
Key facts

Quebec/Canada-based; full Vertical Integration (hardware + software + support).
Covers: lighting, security/alarm, locks/gates, blinds/curtains, multiroom audio, home cinema, video surveillance, water/leak management, robot vacuums/lawnmowers/pool, all everyday IoT.
Customer experience is 100% Plug & Play.
3D/2D real-time interactive home plan is the core visual differentiator.
Local-first: AI, NVR, and automations run on-prem, no cloud dependency for core function.

Related
Strategic Objectives · Target Customers · Three Layer Architecture · ASCIA OS Overview

Vision/Quebec-Market-Focus.title: Quebec Market Focus
Vision/Quebec-Market-Focus.content:
Quebec Market Focus
ASCIA Inc. is based in Montreal, Quebec, Canada. The product targets the Quebec/Canadian premium market first.
Localization

French (priority) and English natively supported across web and mobile.
Mobile UI strings externalized in ARB files (Flutter i18n standard).
RTL prepared for future Arabic expansion.
Date/time/number formatting per device locale.

Local integrations

Hydro-Québec energy tariff integration (peak/off-peak hours, real rates).
Quebec content providers (Club illico, Vrai, Tou.tv) in multimedia.
Public-holiday calendar (Quebec + Canadian).
Quebec Bill 25 compliance for personal data (with GDPR).

Vision/Strategic-Objectives.title: Strategic Objectives
Vision/Strategic-Objectives.content:
Strategic Objectives

Deliver the best home-automation experience on the Quebec/Canadian market for non-technical customers.
Build a 100% proprietary ecosystem — ASCIA hardware + ASCIA software + ASCIA support.
Generate recurring revenue through maintenance subscriptions, updates, and proactive remote support.
Drastically reduce deployment and configuration time for ASCIA technicians.
Differentiate from competitors via the unique 3D Dashboard, advanced Local AI, and unparalleled Plug & Play.

Related
Product Vision · Vertical Integration · Target Customers · Roadmap and Phases

Vision/Target-Customers.title: Target Customers
Vision/Target-Customers.content:
Target Customers
Three primary segments:

Premium Residential — apartments/condos, single-family homes, luxury homes (10–100 devices typical).
Hospitality — boutique hotels (100–500 devices per floor).
Commercial — office buildings, custom projects (500+ devices).

The end user is non-technical and expects the system to work without configuring a modem, router, VLAN, or anything network-related. This drives the entire Plug and Play Philosophy and the rejection of Home Assistant’s developer-oriented UX.
Related
Product Vision · Plug and Play Philosophy · Sales Process · Quebec Market Focus

Vision/Vertical-Integration.title: Vertical Integration
Vision/Vertical-Integration.content:
Vertical Integration
ASCIA designs and manufactures its own hardware (ASCIA Proprietary Hardware), develops its own software (ASCIA OS + Mobile App), and provides on-site installation, training, and ongoing remote support. The full delivery is bundled per deployment workflow.
Components delivered

ASCIA Server (mini-PC pre-installed)
PoE switch(es)
ASCIA Devices (sensors, relays, cameras, audio modules, etc.)
Mobile app (iOS/Android, optional Apple Watch / Wear OS)
Installation by ASCIA technician
Proactive remote support (ASCIA Tele-Diagnosis)

Why
This delivery model is the foundation for the Plug and Play Philosophy and for recurring revenue through maintenance subscriptions.

Product Vision · Sales Process · ASCIA Proprietary Hardware · Hardware Ecosystem Overview

ASCIA OS — Project Vault


                  
                  About 
                  
                

ASCIA OS is a proprietary, premium home-automation operating system developed by ASCIA Inc. (Montreal, Quebec). It is a fork of Home Assistant OS (HAOS) wrapped in three layers (HAOS + ASCIA add-ons + SvelteKit frontend), delivered as a pre-installed disk image on an ASCIA server. The differentiators are a real-time 3D/2D dashboard, Plug & Play deployment, automatic network isolation, local AI, and a Flutter mobile app with deep native integrations.
